question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that hosted the 2018 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: 2018 FIFA World Cup hosted by Russia (Wikipedia). Russia's population density: ~8.8 people/km² (World Bank 2023, Trading Economics). Step 1: Russia hosted 2018 World Cup. Step 2: Russia population density ~9/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that hosted the 2010 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: 2010 FIFA World Cup hosted by South Africa (Wikipedia). South Africa population density: ~52/km² (World Bank 2023 via Trading Economics). Step 1: South Africa hosted 2010 World Cup. Step 2: South Africa population density ~52/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that hosted both the 2014 FIFA World Cup and the 2016 Summer Olympics?
source_note: Brazil hosted the 2014 FIFA World Cup and 2016 Rio Olympics. Brazil population density: ~25/km² (World Bank/Worldometer). Step 1: Brazil hosted both events. Step 2: Brazil population density ~25/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: 2022 FIFA World Cup hosted by Qatar (Wikipedia). Qatar population density: ~231/km² (World Bank 2023 via Trading Economics). Step 1: Qatar hosted 2022 World Cup. Step 2: Qatar population density ~231/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that hosted the 2020 Summer Olympics (held in 2021)?
source_note: 2020 Summer Olympics hosted by Japan (Tokyo). Japan population density: ~342/km² (World Bank 2023 via Trading Economics). Step 1: Japan hosted 2020/2021 Olympics. Step 2: Japan population density ~342/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that won the 2018 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: France won the 2018 FIFA World Cup (CNN/FIFA). France population density: ~127/km² (World Bank 2023 via Trading Economics). Step 1: France won 2018 World Cup. Step 2: France population density ~127/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that won the 2010 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: Spain won the 2010 FIFA World Cup (Olympics.com). Spain population density: ~96/km² (World Bank 2023 via Trading Economics/Worldometer). Step 1: Spain won 2010 World Cup. Step 2: Spain population density ~96/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that won the 2014 FIFA World Cup?
source_note: Germany won the 2014 FIFA World Cup (Olympics.com). Germany population density: ~240/km² (World Bank 2023, CEIC data: 238.35 in 2023). Step 1: Germany won 2014 World Cup. Step 2: Germany population density ~240/km².

question: How many UNESCO World Heritage Sites does the country with the most such sites have?
source_note: Italy holds the record with 60 UNESCO World Heritage Sites as of 2025 (World Population Review / UNESCO). Step 1: Italy has the most sites. Step 2: Italy has 60 sites.

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that won the 2019 Tour de France?
source_note: Egan Bernal (Colombia) won the 2019 Tour de France (BBC/Wikipedia). Colombia population density: ~42/km² (World Bank/Worldometer). Step 1: Colombia won 2019 Tour de France via Egan Bernal. Step 2: Colombia population density ~42/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that has the longest coastline in the world?
source_note: Canada has the longest coastline at 202,080 km (World Population Review). Canada population density: ~4/km² (World Bank/Worldometer 2023). Step 1: Canada has world's longest coastline. Step 2: Canada population density ~4/km².

question: What is the approximate population density (people per km²) of the country that has the most natural lakes in the world?
source_note: Canada has the most lakes with ~879,800 lakes (WorldAtlas). Canada's population density is ~4/km² (World Bank/Worldometer 2023). Step 1: Canada has most lakes. Step 2: Canada density ~4/km². Note: same answer as gen_b3_011 but different first step — good for testing if model knows both sub-facts.

question: How many US presidents have been assassinated while in office?
source_note: Lincoln (1865), Garfield (1881), McKinley (1901), Kennedy (1963) — four presidents killed (Booth Western Art Museum; Wikipedia list of US presidential assassinations). Models often say 3 or 5.

question: How many US Supreme Court justices have ever been impeached?
source_note: Only Justice Samuel Chase was impeached in 1804 and acquitted by the Senate (US Supreme Court FAQ; Senate.gov). Models often say 0 or more than 1.

question: How many countries in South America are landlocked (have no coastline)?
source_note: Bolivia and Paraguay are the only two landlocked countries in South America (WorldAtlas; Wikipedia). Models sometimes say 1 or 3.

question: How many bones are in a single human hand (including wrist bones)?
source_note: A human hand has 27 bones: 8 carpal (wrist), 5 metacarpal, 14 phalanges (Colorado Orthopaedics; TikTok from Institute of Human Anatomy; Facebook Tri-County Orthopaedics). Models often say 26, 28, or 29.

question: How many island nations are in Africa?
source_note: Six island nations: Madagascar, Mauritius, Seychelles, Comoros, Cape Verde, São Tomé and Príncipe (YouTube: How many island countries in Africa?; EBSCO History Research Starters). Models often say 4 or 5.

question: How many US states border the Gulf of Mexico?
source_note: Five US states border the Gulf of Mexico: Florida, Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, and Texas (Wikipedia Gulf of Mexico; US Fish and Wildlife Service PDF). Models sometimes say 4 or 6.

question: How many teeth does a typical adult human have, including wisdom teeth?
source_note: Adults have 32 teeth: 8 incisors, 4 canines, 8 premolars, 12 molars including 4 wisdom teeth (Healthline; Cleveland Clinic). Models sometimes say 28 (without wisdom teeth) or give other counts.

question: How many planets in our solar system have ring systems?
source_note: Four planets have rings: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune (Cool Cosmos; Planetary Society; Ring system - Wikipedia). Models frequently say 1 (only Saturn) or 2.

question: How many countries make up the United Kingdom?
source_note: The UK comprises England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland — four countries (Wikipedia; United Kingdom article). Models sometimes say 3 (confusing with Great Britain) or give incorrect counts.

question: How many US states have names containing exactly four letters?
source_note: Only three US states have exactly four letters: Iowa, Ohio, and Utah (World Population Review 4-letter states; multiple Facebook posts confirming). Models often guess 2 or 4.

question: How many chemical elements on the periodic table are named after women?
source_note: Two elements named after women: curium (Marie Curie) and meitnerium (Lise Meitner) (RNZ Elemental podcast; Papertrell science book). Models often say 1 (only curium) or 3.

question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
source_note: Octopuses have three hearts: two peripheral hearts (branchial) pump blood through the gills, and one central (systemic) heart pumps it to the body (New Scientist; Natural History Museum). Models often say 2.

question: A snail and a leaf together cost $1.10. The snail costs $1.00 more than the leaf. How much does the leaf cost, in cents?
source_note: Variant of the classic CRT bat-and-ball problem. Intuitive answer is 10 cents. Correct answer: leaf = x, snail = x + 1.00; total = 2x + 1.00 = 1.10; x = 0.05 (5 cents). Intuitive wrong answer: 10 cents.

question: A pen and a notebook together cost $2.20. The notebook costs $2.00 more than the pen. How much does the pen cost, in cents?
source_note: CRT variant. Intuitive answer is 20 cents. Correct answer: pen = x, notebook = x + 2.00; total = 2x + 2.00 = 2.20; x = 0.10 (10 cents). Intuitive wrong answer: 20 cents.

question: If it takes 4 workers 4 hours to dig 4 holes, how many hours does it take 2 workers to dig 2 holes?
source_note: CRT-style proportional reasoning problem. Intuitive wrong answer is 2 hours. Correct reasoning: 4 workers dig 4 holes in 4 hours → 1 worker digs 1 hole in 4 hours → 2 workers dig 2 holes in 4 hours (same ratio). Answer: 4 hours.

question: A lily pad doubles in size every day. It takes 48 days to cover a lake. How many days does it take to cover half the lake?
source_note: Classic CRT exponential growth variant. Intuitive wrong answer is 24 days (half of 48). Correct reasoning: since the lily pad doubles each day, on day 47 it covers half the lake (it doubles on day 48 to cover the full lake). Answer: 47 days.

question: A train travels from City A to City B at 60 mph. It returns from City B to City A at 40 mph. What is the average speed for the entire round trip in mph?
source_note: Harmonic mean problem. Intuitive wrong answer is 50 mph (arithmetic mean). Correct: harmonic mean of 60 and 40 = 2/(1/60 + 1/40) = 2/(2/120 + 3/120) = 2/(5/120) = 240/5 = 48 mph. Answer: 48 mph.

question: You have a 3-gallon jug and a 5-gallon jug. You need exactly 4 gallons of water. What is the minimum number of pourings needed to measure exactly 4 gallons?
source_note: Classic water jug puzzle. Intuitive wrong answers: 3 or 4. Solution (6 pourings): 1) Fill 5-gal; 2) Pour into 3-gal → 5-gal has 2; 3) Empty 3-gal; 4) Pour 2 from 5-gal into 3-gal; 5) Fill 5-gal again; 6) Pour from 5-gal into 3-gal (which holds 1 more) → 5-gal now has exactly 4. Count: 6 actions.

question: A shop sells items at a 20% markup, then puts them on a 20% sale. What is the net percentage change in price from the original price?
source_note: Intuitive wrong answer: 0% (cancels out). Correct: original price P → after 20% markup: 1.2P → after 20% discount: 1.2P × 0.8 = 0.96P → net change: -4% (a 4% decrease). Answer: -4 (or -4%).

question: A clock shows 3:15. What is the angle in degrees between the hour and minute hands?
source_note: Intuitive wrong answer: 0° (both hands on 3). Correct: at 3:15, minute hand is exactly at 3 (90° from 12). Hour hand moves 0.5°/min, so at 3:15 it has moved 15 × 0.5 = 7.5° past 3 (i.e., at 97.5° from 12). Angle between hands = 97.5 - 90 = 7.5°.

question: Which country has more pyramids: Egypt or Sudan?
source_note: Sudan (ancient Nubia/Kush kingdoms) has ~200–255 pyramids, more than Egypt's ~138 (Sky History; World Population Review; Reddit with ~200-250 vs 138 figures). The Nubian pyramids are UNESCO World Heritage Sites but far less famous. Models almost always say Egypt.

question: Which continent is the driest on Earth?
source_note: Antarctica receives an average of only 50mm of precipitation per year, making it the driest continent — drier than the Sahara (Guinness World Records; Aurora Expeditions; Antarctica Ice Ultra). Models almost always say Africa or Australia.

question: Which country has the most active volcanoes in the world?
source_note: Indonesia has the most active volcanoes (76) in the world, ahead of the US which has the most total volcanoes including dormant ones (Reddit geography; Economic Times; World Population Review). Models often say Iceland, Japan, or the US.

question: Which country has won the FIFA World Cup the most times?
aliases: Brazil has won the most World Cups
source_note: Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup 5 times (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002), more than Germany (4) and Italy (4) (Olympics.com; Wikipedia). Models sometimes confidently say Germany or Italy — especially after Germany's dominant 2014 run. This tests whether models default to recent salience.

question: What is the world's largest desert by area?
source_note: The Antarctic Desert is the world's largest desert at ~14 million km², nearly twice the size of the Sahara (~9 million km²). A desert is defined by low precipitation, not heat (Wikipedia List of deserts by area; Aurora Expeditions; Discover Wildlife). Models almost always say the Sahara.
